front: What is Capacity Planning in IT infrastructure?
back: The process of determining the resources (CPU, memory, storage, network) needed to meet current and future workload demands while maintaining service quality and controlling costs.

front: What is the difference between Tactical and Strategic Capacity Planning?
back: Tactical planning focuses on short-term adjustments (days to months) like scaling resources; Strategic planning involves long-term (months to years) capacity roadmaps and infrastructure investments.
The minimum guaranteed level of resources (CPU, memory, bandwidth) that is always available to handle expected normal workload conditions.

front: Define Utilization Threshold in capacity planning
back: A predefined percentage of resource usage (typically 70-80%) that triggers alerts or scaling actions to prevent performance degradation before capacity is exhausted.
Predicting future resource requirements based on historical data analysis, growth trends, seasonal patterns, and business projections to plan capacity in advance.

front: Define Service Level Agreement (SLA) in capacity context
back: A contract specifying minimum performance metrics (response time, uptime, throughput) that the IT infrastructure must meet, directly influencing capacity requirements.
The process of optimizing resource configurations (system parameters, application settings) to maximize performance from existing capacity before adding more resources.
Vertical scaling: adding more power (CPU/RAM) to existing servers. Horizontal scaling: adding more servers to distribute load across multiple machines.
A component (CPU, disk I/O, network, memory) that limits overall system performance because it operates at or near 100% utilization, constraining throughput.
Average time a system operates before experiencing a failure; used in capacity planning to estimate reliability and plan for redundancy.

front: What is the difference between Capacity and Performance?
back: Capacity refers to the maximum workload a system can handle (throughput limit); Performance measures how well the system handles its current workload (response time, latency).
Additional temporary capacity (often cloud-based) that can be automatically provisioned during peak demand periods beyond normal capacity limits.

front: What is the Capacity Planning Lifecycle?
back: Four phases: 1) Monitor - collect metrics, 2) Analyze - identify trends, 3) Plan - forecast needs, 4) Implement - provision resources.

front: Define Right-Sizing in cloud capacity planning
back: Matching allocated cloud resources to actual usage patterns, eliminating over-provisioning (wasting money) and under-provisioning (performance issues).
